name: Blueberries

book_claims.claim: Wild blueberries and blackberries contain highest phenolic phytochemical content among fruits, contributing to lower inflammation
book_claims.mechanism: Anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins with potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity

supplementary_research.finding: A 2019 systematic review and meta-analysis (Nutrients, PMID 31329250) of RCTs found that blueberry supplementation significantly reduced systolic blood pressure and LDL cholesterol, supporting cardiovascular benefits.

A 2019 RCT in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (PMID 30725213) found that consuming 150g/day of blueberries for 6 months improved vascular function and reduced systolic blood pressure in metabolic syndrome participants, mediated by improved endothelial function.

Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study (Circulation, 2013, PMID 23319811) found that higher anthocyanin intake (primarily from blueberries and strawberries) was associated with a 32% reduced risk of myocardial infarction in young and middle-aged women.

Examine.com summary: blueberries have strong evidence for improving blood pressure, cognitive function in older adults, and vascular function. Anthocyanins are the primary bioactive compounds. Benefits appear dose-dependent with 150-300g fresh blueberries daily.

A 2019 systematic review (Nutritional Neuroscience, PMID 31383694) found that blueberry supplementation improved memory performance in older adults with mild cognitive impairment, consistent with neuroprotective effects of anthocyanins crossing the blood-brain barrier.

seasonality: Peak season June through August in Northern Hemisphere; frozen available year-round. Wild (lowbush) blueberries typically harvested late July to early September.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Low oral bioavailability (~1-2% absorbed intact), but gut microbial metabolites (phenolic acids) are well absorbed and may account for much of the biological activity. Absorption improved when consumed with fat.
Polyphenol content highest in wild varieties. Freezing preserves most polyphenol content.
Good bioavailability; contributes to but is not the primary driver of antioxidant capacity in blueberries.
Resveratrol analogue with 4x higher bioavailability than resveratrol due to methyl groups increasing lipophilicity. Crosses blood-brain barrier.
Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber; contributes to prebiotic effects supporting gut microbiome diversity.
